coredata.dc:title: White light emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting applications of microwave assisted synthesized green fluorescent carbon dots derived from waste curry leaves

coredata.dc:description: We have prepared waste curry leaves extract derived carbon dots (CL-C dots) by microwave assisted method. The synthesized CL-C dots were well characterized by many techniques. Using HR-TEM images, the size of the synthesized CL-C dots were determined to be 3.55 nm. CL-C dots exhibits green fluorescence and obtained emission maximum at 425 nm upon the excitation at 340 nm. The green emissive CL-C dots quantum yield (QY) was determined to be 27.13%. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was, as calculated as 1.1 from the Raman spectrum. Moreover, CL-C dots were successfully applied for the preparation of the White Light Emitting Diode (WLED) device. The developed WLED has a colour coordinate value of (0.307, 0.354) and a colour correlated temperature of 6617 K. Finally, we have been used CL-C dots for anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink application, it is a low-cost, simple-to-use substance that will eventually replace commercially available fluorescent inks. This research work offers a vision in the fields of materials science, WLED, energy, anti-counterfeiting and forensic science.

head.ce:title: White light emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting applications of microwave assisted synthesized green fluorescent carbon dots derived from waste curry leaves

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have prepared waste curry leaves extract derived carbon dots (CL-C dots) by microwave assisted method. The synthesized CL-C dots were well characterized by many techniques. Using HR-TEM images, the size of the synthesized CL-C dots were determined to be 3.55 nm. CL-C dots exhibits green fluorescence and obtained emission maximum at 425 nm upon the excitation at 340 nm. The green emissive CL-C dots quantum yield (QY) was determined to be 27.13%. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was, as calculated as 1.1 from the Raman spectrum. Moreover, CL-C dots were successfully applied for the preparation of the White Light Emitting Diode (WLED) device. The developed WLED has a colour coordinate value of (0.307, 0.354) and a colour correlated temperature of 6617 K. Finally, we have been used CL-C dots for anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink application, it is a low-cost, simple-to-use substance that will eventually replace commercially available fluorescent inks. This research work offers a vision in the fields of materials science, WLED, energy, anti-counterfeiting and forensic science.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are a new branch of carbon nanomaterials having a size less than 10 nm in diameter (Sciortino et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Baker and Baker, 2010). It has attracted considerable interest for the past few years. They are comprised of well-organised carbon atoms with a high aspect ratio, high surface area, high thermal and chemical stabilities. It has functional group including hydroxyl, carboxyl, aldehyde and epoxide etc. Carbon dots formed by sp2 and sp3 hybridization carbon probes, which it can be easy to surface modification (Mishra et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015a). Compared with other semiconductor quantum dots such as cadmium sulfide (CdS), cadmium selenide (CdSe), zinc sulfide (ZnS), zinc oxide (ZnO) and organic fluorescent dyes, carbon dots have unique optical and chemical properties in particular excellent stability, high aqueous solubility, bio-compatibility and high luminescence (Permatasari et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2009; Kasouni et al., 2021). More importantly, properties of carbon dots can be able to tune by changing their starting materials, pH and temperature. Because of these excellent properties of carbon dots, it is extensively used in several fields including bio-imaging, sensing, drug delivery, printing ink, solar cells, optoelectronics, catalysis, LED and supercapacitor (Gao et al., 2018; Zhang and Fan, 2021; Yin et al., 2021).

Carbon dots have attracted considerable interest in recent days. Because the precursor for carbon dots synthesis is abundant, renewable, clean, non-toxic, and diverse. The green synthesis of carbon dots is an alternative method for chemical synthesis (Das et al., 2018; Qu et al., 2020). Nowadays, several reports exist on the synthesis of carbon dots from natural sources such as milk, turmeric, cinnamon, black pepper, red chili and even some other waste materials (Vasimalai et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016). It is imperative to explore a new carbon source that processes abundant results with simple operation and an environment friendly synthesis method. Reported articles have revealed that the synthesized C-dots from waste or renewable source shows amazing physical, chemical and medicinal properties (Naik et al., 2020; Mathew et al., 2019).

It is well known that several methods are available for the synthesis of carbon dots including solvothermal (Zhao et al., 2018), hydrothermal method (Lu et al., 2012), laser ablation (Kaczmarek et al., 2021), ball-milling (Wang et al., 2015b), UV-light assisted method (Costas-Mora et al., 2015), microwave assisted method (Pires et al., 2015) and ultrasonication method (Ma et al., 2012) etc. Among them, the synthesis of nanomaterials using the microwave assisted method, particularly carbon dots have been receiving enamours attention for past few decades (Gayen et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2009). Microwave assisted method is a time saving, economical, eco-friendly, energy efficient method to synthesis of nanomaterials (Costas-Mora et al., 2015; Pires et al., 2015). The carbonization process occurs very quickly by using the microwave method (Ma et al., 2012).

These days, light emitting diode (LED) has received much attention in energy saving point of view. LED is made up of PN junction semiconducting material, when forward voltage is applied to LED, the electron from the conduction band recombines with the hole in the valance band. These kinds of recombination produce electroluminescence (Loi et al., 2021). LEDs are fabricated by semiconducting materials. It has several advantages including a longer lifetime, improved physical, chemical properties and smaller in size (Dodaro, 1991; Allen and Steckl, 2008). It is extensively used in several fields including indicators in science, lighting, data communication, mission vision system, biological, toxic chemical sensor, costume for stage performance, LED display and LED panel light source etc., (Krames et al., 2007; Cheng and Cheng et al., 2006). In particularly, White Light Emitting Diode (WLED) has been used as a commercial light source. Carbon dots are a promising alternative and cost effective material to prepare WLED and they are used as a colour conversion layer in WLED. Carbon dots based LEDs have many advantages such as cheap cost material, high lifetime, enhanced stability, good photo stability and less photo-bleaching effect (Sheu et al., 2003; Ding et al., 2019; Paulo-Mirasol et al., 2019; Singh and Mishra, 2016). This demonstrates that carbon dots are a potential candidate for preparation of WLED (Krames et al., 2007; Cheng and Cheng, 2006). It is well known that the existing fluorescence nature and cytotoxicity of carbon dots is based on their starting material [Liu et al., 2021]. Many reports are available in the literature, which shows the less cytotoxicity and more bio-compatibility with normal cells [Dias et al., 2019; Chan et al., 2021; Edison et al., 2016], which are all have been synthesized by plant and natural source. These carbon dots also used for bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensor, anti-inflammatory and skin wound healing applications. We kept this thing in mind and used waste curry leaves as source for the synthesis of carbon dots by microwave method.

Curry leave (Murraya Koenigii) plant can grow up to 1500 m and it is abundantly found in India, Pakistan, Russia, China, Sri Lanka and Myanmar (Batool et al., 2020; Steinhaus 2015). These curry leaves have cooling in nature, showing analgesic and anthelmintic effects. It reduces the body heat, skin etching and kidney disorderness. Curry leaves contain β-carotene, menthone, menthol, carvomenthone, flavonoids and actinides (Batool et al., 2020; Mittal, 2017). They are rich in nicotinic acid, vitamin A, vitamin C, minerals and calcium. In particularly, 100 g of curry leaves contains 6 g protein, 1 g fat, carbohydrates 18.7 g, calcium 830 mg, iron 0.3 mg, β-carotene 7560 μg. Curry leaves far used to produce bathing soaps, facial creams, hair treatment (Batool et al., 2020; Plant et al., 2015).

Counterfeiting is a major problem in the world that represents a danger to a person and society. Over the past decade, counterfeit products have been spread from daily consumer goods to medicines and high-tech products (Liu et al., 2019a; Carro-Temboury et al., 2018). In spite of the fact that a larger part of the items is secured by an enemy of duplicating strategy, the worldwide financial loss of duplicating has been expanding yearly and assessed to arrive at 1.7 trillion US dollars in 2015 (Bascap, 2011). Thus, there are serious environmental, health, economic, and social effects that counterfeiting in its wake. This accelerated growth of forgeries, give challenge to researchers globally to come up with innovative and extremely secure anti-counterfeiting techniques to combat the issue (Liu et al., 2019a; Kwok et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink can effectively avoid counterfeit, shoddy things due to its high throughput, easy handling, and simple design (Kelesidis and Falagas, 2015; Singh et al., 2010).

Carbon dots based anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink has several advantages such as low toxicity, high fluorescent nature, easy handling, simple design and waste to value. It is suitable for food safety, biology, security and emergency information exchange. They are used for fast, frequent identification of counterfeiting, information encryption and information storage applications.

We kept the above mentioned objectives in our mind and prepared C-dots from waste curry leaves by microwave assisted method. Herein, we are reporting the synthesis of curry leaves derived carbon dots and their use for WLED, anti-counterfeiting applications. Synthesized curry leaves derived carbon dots (CL-C dots) was well characterized by several techniques. Moreover, we have utilized CL-C dots for the development of WLED device. Finally, we have used CL-C dots for anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink applications.

Waste curry leaves were collected from our institute canteen. Ethanol, quinine sulfate, polyvinylpyrrolidone, sodium chloride, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, disodium phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were received from Sigma-Aldrich. pH buffers were prepared from pH 4 to pH 10 by adjusting their pH using HCl and NaOH. UV-LED (5 mm; 395–400 nm) and HW battery 8 V were purchased from the nearby Hardware shop. Bank currency notes (Artificial currency notes) were purchased from the stationery shop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of curry leaves derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Curry leaves were ground by a household mixer grinder to prepare curry leaves extract. Then, the resultant extract was diluted for 60 mL of ultrapure water and the diluted solution was transferred to 250 mL, borosilicate beaker. The beaker was placed into the microwave oven and heated at 650 W for 8 min (Scheme. 1 ). After 8 min of reaction time, the brownish-black solution was obtained with a black precipitate. It has removed by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant solution has filtered by cellulose (0.22 μm) filter paper. The resultant CL-C dots were stored in the refrigerator.

Absorption spectral studies have been carried out by UV–visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 25). A spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer lambda LS45) was used to analyze the spectrum emission of fluorescence. Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy spectral analyses were performed by (JASCO 6300) FT-IR spectrometer. TEM images were imaged by Transmission Electron Microscopy (JEOL-2100) and operated condition at 200 kV. The X-ray diffraction pattern were studies have performed by Rigaku XRD using (λ = 1.5406) Ni-Filtered CuKα radiation. Confocal Raman Spectroscopy Alpha 300R (WiTech GmbH, Germany) was used to perform Raman spectral analysis. Microwave oven (LG 20L Solo) with electrical power 800 W operating voltage 234 V was used for the preparation of CL-C dots. The digital images were taken with the camera of (12MP + 2MP dual rear camera) Redmi 7 smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of chlorophyll from waste curry leaves
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of waste curry leaves were taken and cut into small pieces. It has been transferred into a 100 mL beaker. In the same beaker, 15 mL ethanol was added and allowed to leach chlorophyll for 12 h. The extracted chlorophyll solution was filtered by Whatman filter paper. We used curry leaves extracted chlorophyll for WLED application without any additional purification.

20 μL of green emissive CL-C dots (14 mg/mL), 100 μL of blue emissive quinine sulfate (2 mg/mL) and 100 μL of red emissive chlorophyll (7 mg/mL) were mixed together in optimized ratio of 1:5:5. The Optimized ratio of white emissive solutions were mixed with polyvinylpyrrolidone solution. The obtained solution was coated in a PET cap and kept 12 hr to dry. After that PET cap was illuminated by 395 nm near UV-LED that has operating voltage 1.5 V and it shows white emission.

Invisible anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink was prepared by mixing curry leaves derived green fluorescent carbon dots with 0.1% of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) solution in 2:1 ratio to form a viscous solution. It has been used as an ink in marker. Using that marker, we have done writing and drawing on Whatman No 1. filter paper. Then the filter paper was dried for 5 min at room temperature. Finally, we have taken a photographs under day and UV lights.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible and fluorescence spectrum of CL-C dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible spectrum of CL-C dots shows in Fig. S1. The CL-C dots humps were obtained at 276 and 325 nm, which refers the transition of ᴨ → ᴨ* (CC) and the transition of n → ᴨ* (CO & CO), respectively (Permatasari et al., 2016; Dias et al., 2019). Fig. 1 (A) shows the broad fluorescence spectrum of CL-C dots. The observed fluorescence spectrum is very broad range from 355 to 650 nm and it attained maximum intensity at 425 nm (λex: 340 nm) (Shahshahanipour et al., 2019; Sharma et al., 2016; Ensafi et al., 2017). In daylight, the colour of CL-C dots seemed yellow (Fig. 1(A) Inset (i)) and appeared green under UV light (Fig. 1(A) Inset (ii)). The origin of the fluorescence behaviour of C-dots are due to the defect state emission (surface energy traps) and intrinsic state emission (electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect/zigzag sites). The intrinsic state emission causes the blue emission (shorter wavelength) and defect states emission gives the green emission (longer wavelength), which were confirmed by the fluorescence colour of C-dots (Sharma et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2015). Due to this reason, our CL-C dots have green fluorescent. Whereas the absorbance of curry leaves extract, shows a sharp absorption peak at 302 nm and small humps at 440, 483 and 676 nm (Fig. S2). These peak and humps have not obtained in the absorbance spectrum of CL-C dots (Fig. S1). The colour of the curry leaves extract observed green in day light (Fig. S2. Inset). These findings are confirmed as successful synthesis of green fluorescent C-dots.

Further, we have examined the emission spectral study of CL-C dots with different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 1(B), exhibits a 3D plot of emission spectra of CL-C dots with different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 400 nm. The emission intensity was increased, when increase the excitation wavelength from 270 to 340 nm. Whereas the fluorescence emission maximum attain at 425 nm (λex = 340 nm) (Fig. 1(B)). Beyond the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, the emission intensity was quenched and the red shift was observed (Fig. 1(B)). Few articles are available the literature to explain about red shift properties of carbon dots. Furthermore, the presence of distinct emissive sites on the surface, as well as the abundance of carboxyl functional groups on the surface, leads in variable emission behaviour. Despite the large volume of research in this field, the mechanism of PL in C-dots remains uncertain (Sharma et al., 2016; Roy et al., 2015; Xiao et al., 2017). Fig. S3, shows CIE (Commission Internationale De l’Eclairage) chromaticity diagram of CL-C dots. The acquired results coordinates of (0.288, 0.643), which confirmed the CL-C dots has green emitting nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectra of curry leaves extract and CL-C dots
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, we have studied the functional group of curry leaves extract and CL-C dots by FT-IR spectral technique (Fig. S4). Fig. S4, (Curve a), shows FT-IR spectrum of curry leaves extract observed CH vibrational stretching and OH vibrational stretching at 2934 cm−1 and 3315 cm−1, respectively (Vasimalai et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2010; Huck, 2015). The functional groups of CO, CO and CH, vibrational stretching peaks observed at 1592, 1060 and 1390 cm−1, respectively (Fig. S4. Curve a) (Vasimalai et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2010; Huck, 2015; Singh et al., 2015). Fig. S4 (Curve b), shows the FT-IR spectrum of CL-C dots. All similar peaks of curry leaves were retained in the FT-IR spectrum of CL-C dots however, OH, CO peaks intensity get increased. These findings are confirmed the successful formation of C-dots having hydroxy, carboxylic and epoxide present on the surface by CL-C dots.

Fig. 2 , shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of CL-C dots. HR-TEM image shows spherical like shape and monodispersed CL-C dots (Fig. 2(A)). The average diameter of the CL-C dots were calculated to be 3.55 ± 0.03 nm using a particles size histogram (Fig. 2(B)). The result was demonstrating CL-C dots spherical like shape and quantum size particle, it is consistent with previous work (Sciortino et al., 2018, Vasimalai et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD pattern and Raman spectra of CL-C dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (A) shows the XRD pattern of CL-C dots. The XRD peaks were obtained at 21.51°θ and 39.64°θ, which are the corresponding planes of (002) and (100), respectively. There were no sharp peaks obtained in the XRD data. The observed peaks revealed that the synthesised CL-C dots are amorphous, which is consistent with prior reports (Teng et al., 2014; Bajpai et al., 2019).

It is very important to characterize the D band and G band of C-dots through Raman spectral characterization to find out the structural defect. Fig. 3(B) observed the Raman spectrum of CL-C dots, D-band and G-band were observed at 1341 and 1559 cm−1, respectively. The resulting G band (sp2) arises from the E2g vibrational mode of sp2 carbon and the “D” band (sp3) arises from the A1g symmetry photons near the K-zone boundary [14,59,60]. The intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) was calculated to be 1.1. The obtain intensity ratio value is revealed that the synthesized CL-C dots having surface defect on their surface (Vasimalai et al., 2018; Hosseini et al., 2011; Chhabra et al., 2018).

The Quantum Yield of CL-C dots were calculated using a comparison of the wavelength integrated intensity method of the unknown sample with a standard. Quinine sulfate was used as the reference QY standard (QY = 0.54) (Rhys Williams et al., 1983; Melhuish, 1961; Instruments, 2000). The quantum yield value (QYx) of CL-C dots were determined by using William’s comparative method. The QYx value was calculated according to the following equation (1).
(1)
QYx =
Fx × A s × Q Y s
Fs × A x
where “As” represent absorbance at the excitation wavelength of quinine sulfate, “Fs” symbolize integrated fluorescence area quinine sulfate, “Ax” symbolize absorbance at the excitation wavelength of CL-C dots, “Fx” represent integrated fluorescence emission of CL-C dots, “QYx” is the quantum yield of CL-C dots, “QYs” is the quantum yield of the standard quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54)) (Rhys Williams et al., 1983; Melhuish, 1961). Finally, the quantum yield of CL-C dots were calculated to be 27.13 % using William’s comparative method and this value is revealed that the synthesised CL-C dots have highly fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ionic strength and effect of pH
ce:section.ce:para: We have performed an ionic strength study based on the NaCl concentration. Fig. S5, shows the plot of concentration NaCl versus fluorescence intensity. When the sodium chloride concentration was adjusted from 0 to 25 mM, the fluorescence intensity of CL-C dots did not change (Fig. S5). Beyond the 25 mM of NaCl concentration, the fluorescence intensity was quenched due to the neutral condition of CL-C dots and which let to aggregated the nanoparticles. The synthesized CL-C dots have the tolerance ionic strength level up to 25 mM. Hence, we concluded that our CL-C dots are stable even in high ionic strength medium.

We also have studied the effect of pH for CL-C dots. The fluorescence intensity was high from pH 4 to 7 (Fig. S6). Beyond, pH 8 emission intensity was quenched. We expecting that the presence of an excess of alkaline (OH–), decreased the fluorescence intensity of CL-C dots. The observed high fluorescence intensity from pH 4 to 7 due to the higher net surface charge and higher charge endows the CL-C dots. CL-C dots with hydrophilic properties and stability which is consequently enhancing the fluorescence intensity (Jin et al., 2015). Therefore, synthesised CL-C dots were stable at pH range from 3 to7.

We have prepared WLED by mixing chlorophyll, quinine sulfate and CL-C dots (Experimental section 2.5). First, we have characterized optical properties of chlorophyll and quinine sulfate. Fig. S7 and Fig. S8(A) show UV–visible and fluorescence spectrum of chlorophyll. The absorbance peaks were observed at 411, 507, 538, 608 and 664 nm. When it is excited at 430 nm, the fluorescence intensity was obtained at 530 and 686 nm (Pedrós et al., 2008). Under day and UV light, chlorophyll seems green and dark red colour, respectively (Fig. S8A. Inset (i) and (ii)). Fig. S8B shows CIE chromaticity diagram of chlorophyll. The acquired results coordinates of (0.594, 0.406), which confirmed the chlorophyll has red emitting nature.

UV–visible and fluorescence spectrum of quinine sulfate is shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10(A). The absorbance peaks were observed at 317 and 348 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained at 441 nm upon the excitation (317 nm) of quinine sulphate. Fig. S10(A) Inset (i) and (ii) shows photographs quinine sulfate seems colourless under day light and under UV light it observed bright blue colour. Fig. S10(B) shows CIE chromaticity diagram of quinine sulfate. The obtained chromaticity coordinate of quinine sulfate is (0.201, 0.151). It was confirmed quinine sulfate has blue emitting nature.

Green emissive material CL-C dots, blue emissive quinine sulfate and red emissive chlorophyll were mixed together in the optimized ratio of 1:5:5 and the obtained solution gives white colour under UV-light. Fluorescence spectra of white emissive solution shows overlapping peaks at 437, 490, 528 and 676 nm for excitation wavelength 395 nm, due to the presence of CL-C dots, quinine sulfate and chlorophyll mixture (Fig. 4 (A)). Fig. 4(B) shows that the CIE chromaticity diagram of white emissive solution, the obtained chromaticity coordinate of white emissive solution is (0.307, 0.354) with a colour correlated temperature (CCT) value of 6617 K. Fig. 4B. Inset shows photographs of white emissive solution under UV light. Chromaticity coordinates of our white emissive solution nearly matchs with coordinates of commercial WLED (0.33, 0.33) (Ding et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Moreover, we have carried out a photostability for our white emissive solution (Fig. S11). The photostability study shows, higher stability in fluorescence intensity even after 336 hr under the illumine of Xenon light at 395 nm.

Further, the optimized ratio of 1:5:5 white emissive solution mixed with polyvinylpyrrolidone solution. The polymer mixed solution was coated in a PET cap and kept 12 hrs to dry. After that PET cap was illuminated by 395 nm near UV-LED (1.5 V) it shows white emission. (Fig. 4(A). Inset). The white emission was obtained from mixture of three different emissions (Red, green and blue). This White LED device will be a beneficial in the field of nanotechnology, optoelectronics and material sciences.

Further, we have carried out stability study for anti-Counterfeiting fluorescence ink coated Whatman filter paper and bank currency notes (Fig. S12). Even beyond 4-week “information icon and bank currency note” green fluorescent symbols visible under the UV light. Our Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink will be a very useful material to write any confidential matter for army/security purpose, emergency and society relevant applications.

According to the literature review report (Table 1 ), there are just a few publications in the literature that indicate high quantum yield. However, such methods have several drawbacks, such as a lengthy synthesis procedure, chemical synthesis, a high temperature requirement, and a high cost. Nevertheless, the present method having the following novelties (i) waste to value technology, (ii) synthesis time is 8 min, (iii) low cost, (iv) high quantum yield, (v) successfully developed white LED device and (vi) CL-C dots applied for anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink applications.

We have successfully prepared waste curry leaves extract derived green fluorescent CL-C dots by microwave assisted method. The synthesized CL-C dots were well characterized several techniques. The synthesized CL-C dots particle sizes was calculated to be 3.55 nm using HR-TEM images. The quantum yield of the green fluorescent CL-C dots are calculated to be 27.13%. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated to be 1.1 from Raman spectrum and conforming that CL-C dots have defects on their surface. Green fluorescent CL-C dots were applied for the preparation of WLED by mixing with chlorophyll, quinine sulfate and WLED device was successfully prepared. The prepared WLED has colour coordinates value (0.307, 0.354) and colour correlated temperature 6617 K, which was well matched with ideal WLED device. Finally, we have used CL-C dots for anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink application. This work offers a vision in the field of material sciences, LED, anti-counterfeiting and forensics science.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
